results.title: EgoMem: Lifelong Memory Agent for Full-duplex Omnimodal Models
results.content:
detects dialog boundaries from omnimodal streams, and extracts necessary information to update the long-term memory. Unlike existing memory agents for LLMs, EgoMem relies entirely on raw audiovisual streams, making it especially suitable for lifelong, real-time, and embodied scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that EgoMem’s retrieval and memory management modules achieve over 95% accuracy on the test set. When integrated with a fine-tuned RoboEgo omnimodal chatbot, the system achieves fact-consistency scores above 87% in real-time personalized dialogs, establishing a strong baseline for future research. [...] Abstract: We introduce EgoMem, the first lifelong memory agent tailored for full-duplex models that process real-time omnimodal streams. EgoMem enables real-time models to recognize multiple users directly from raw audiovisual streams, to provide personalized response, and to maintain long-term knowledge of users’ facts, preferences, and social relationships extracted from audiovisual history. EgoMem operates with three asynchronous processes: (i) a retrieval process that dynamically identifies user via face and voice, and gathers relevant context from a long-term memory; (ii) an omnimodal dialog process that generates personalized audio responses based on the retrieved context; and (iii) a memory management process that automatically detects dialog boundaries from omnimodal streams, and [...] back arrowGo to ICLR 2026 Conference homepage

## EgoMem: Lifelong Memory Agent for Full-duplex Omnimodal Models

Download PDF

### Yiqun Yao, Naitong Yu, Xiang Li, Xin Jiang, Xuezhi Fang, Wenjia Ma, Xuying Meng, Jing Li, Aixin Sun, Yequan Wang

19 Sept 2025 (modified: 11 Feb 2026)Submitted to ICLR 2026EveryoneRevisionsBibTeXCC BY 4.0

Keywords: Memory Agent, Multimodal, Full-duplexity, LLM

TL;DR: The first memory agent for full-duplex omnimodal models that delivers lifelong, personalized, real-time spoken dialog

results.title: Verification of Embodied Agents - Guided Action Selection ... - note
results.content:
### Impact on us

This research forces a fundamental revision of the design philosophy for next-generation multimodal AI currently competing in development. In the future, there may be a shift toward architectures that can directly output action spaces corresponding to each modality (e.g., generating robot movements directly from visual input) rather than omnimodal models that simply 'translate all inputs into text.' For developers, the lesson is that when incorporating existing VLMs or LLMs into agents, one should not easily assume that 'because it understands, it will act,' but rather that the quality of action generation must be verified independently. This is considered an opportunity to encourage the activation of the new field of action-centric multimodal learning. [...] While omnimodal LLMs, which integrate and process all modalities such as images, audio, video, and text, are expected to be the next generation of AI, this paper reveals their fundamental weakness. As the paper title 'Senses Wide Shut' suggests, it argues that while these models can 'perceive' (representation) diverse inputs, they possess a 'Representation-Action Gap' where they cannot convert them into appropriate 'actions.' In experiments targeting multiple omnimodal models, the authors demonstrated that even if models appear to understand inputs correctly, they show significantly lower performance in actual action selection tasks compared to humans or specialized models. This is not merely a lack of performance but may stem from structural design flaws in multimodal integration [...] Today's AI technology trends focus on the action reliability and self-expansion capabilities of Embodied Agents that interact with the physical world, as well as the fundamental structural challenges of multimodal large language models. In particular, approaches that insert evaluations by a Verifier before action execution to reduce errors, and frameworks where agents actively acquire unknown tools through hierarchical skill trees, represent significant steps toward the practical application of autonomous agents. Meanwhile, the representation-action gap in omni-modal LLMs, where 'perception does not lead to action,' has become apparent, necessitating a rethink of the design philosophy of multimodal AI itself. results.title: Embodied AI Agents: Modeling the World - arXiv
results.content: In this paper, we first give an overview of different agent types and their applications, we then describe the framework proposed for embodied AI agents, namely world models, which includes perception, physical and mental world modeling, memory and action and control. We describe world models based on generative models as well as alternative predictive world models that are potentially more efficient and trustworthy. We then describe the capabilities and models for three types of embodied AI agents, namely (1) virtual embodied Agents; (2) wearable agents; and (3) robotic agents. In each section, we describe benchmarks for, as well as potential future research directions in each agent type. Finally, we describe a future vision of embodied learning, and a Family of Agents that all work with [...] To act in the physical world an embodied agent must understand this world. Planning is the practical reason we care about world models. An embodied agent that must achieve an explicit task — fix a bicycle, fold laundry, guide a user through a recipe—cannot rely on deterministic or memorized plans alone, it must imagine how the environment will evolve under alternative actions (and changing environments and intent), score those imagined futures, and pick the sequence with the lowest expected cost. Supervised behaviour cloning can only imitate what it has seen; Reinforcement Learning (RL) requires trial-and-error in the real physical environment and explicit verifiable rewards which is extremely difficult to gather. In comparison, LeCun’s AMI architecture (LeCun, 2022) lays out a clear [...] Meta researchers are working on different hypotheses, including an alternative world modeling based on transformer and JEPA architectures, as described in Section 5.2 below. This approach has the potential to enable more efficient and effective long horizon action planning, allowing wearable devices to provide more sophisticated assistance to users. Another approach is to train VLMs to directly predict a wearer’s goals based on ego-centric context. To test this new set of models researchers created a benchmark of different scenarios that provide the needed contextual cues to perform goal inference.

results.title: Embodied agent - Wikipedia
results.content:
In artificial intelligence, an embodied agent, also sometimes referred to as an interface agent, is an intelligent agent that interacts with the environment through a physical body within that environment. Agents that are represented graphically with a body, for example a human or a cartoon animal, are also called embodied agents, although they have only virtual, not physical, embodiment. A branch of artificial intelligence focuses on empowering such agents to interact autonomously with human beings and the environment. Mobile robots are one example of physically embodied agents; Ananova and Microsoft Agent are examples of graphically embodied agents. Embodied conversational agents are embodied agents (usually with a graphical front-end as opposed to a robotic body) that are capable of [...] ## Embodied conversational agents

Embodied conversational agents are a form of intelligent user interface. Graphically embodied agents aim to unite gesture, facial expression and speech to enable face-to-face communication with users, providing a powerful means of human-computer interaction.

## Advantages [...] Research has found that users prefer a non-verbal visual indication of an embodied system's internal state to a verbal indication, demonstrating the value of additional non-verbal communication channels. As well as this, the face-to-face communication involved in interacting with an embodied agent can be conducted alongside another task without distracting the human participants, instead improving the enjoyment of such an interaction. Furthermore, the use of an embodied presentation agent results in improved recall of the presented information.

In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, pages 14281–14290, 2024.
 Edward J Hu, Yelong Shen, Phillip Wallis, Zeyuan Allen-Zhu, Yuanzhi Li, Shean Wang, Lu Wang, and Weizhu Chen.
Lora: Low-rank adaptation of large language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.09685, 2021. [...] 1. Introduction Foundation Models have demonstrated broad capabilities across language and image understanding tasks [5, 16, 30, 33, 42, 43, 45, 46, 51, 58, 66, 95, 103]. In particular, Mul-timodal LLMs (MLLMs) – multimodal foundation models trained on vast amounts of textual and image data – excel at tasks that are natural to their text and image training modal-ities. As an extension of MLLMs, Vision-Language-Action models have been successfully applied in robotics and em-bodied AI [3, 11, 18, 80], as well as agents for the web [27, 37, 75, 102] and user interface (UI) control [28, 68, 89]. [...] agent can be trained to be generally proﬁcient in all of these domains. This is a challenging problem as many of these tasks require physics and geometric reasoning, their embod-iments are either static or share morphologies via a mobile manipulator, their applications require long-horizon plan-ning, and many are partially observable and require reason-ing over long sequences of observations. In addition, train-ing with combined data across domains with these similar-ities may yield cross-domain beneﬁts, where a single agent may outperform agents trained on individual domains.
